name: Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares
name_original: Llanura de las Cuevas del Campo — circular por el Embalse de Negratín
description: Cycle the moderate 39.1-mile Plain of the Caves of Campo route, enjoying turquoise Negratín Reservoir views and unique geological formations
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Freila traverse a diverse landscape in Granada, Spain, characterized by expansive reservoir waters, reddish wastelands, and fertile valleys. The region features semi-desert badlands and olive groves, offering varied backdrops for cycling. Prominent hills like Cerro Jabalcón provide challenging ascents and panoramic views. This terrain creates a network of road cycling routes suitable for various fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Freila The most popular road cycling route is Pico Jabalcón and Negratín Reservoir Loop, a 23.0 miles (36.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 30 minutes to complete. This route offers continuous views of the reservoir and includes significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares, a moderate 39.1 miles (62.9 km) path. This route circles the expansive Negratín Reservoir, showcasing its turquoise waters and surrounding arid hills. Local road cyclists also love the Baza – Historic Center of Baza loop from Baza, a 47.5 miles (76.5 km) trail leading through the historic town of Baza, often completed in about 3 hours 54 minutes. Road cycling around Freila is defined by the Negratín Reservoir, reddish badlands, and the hills of Cerro Jabalcón. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Freila are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Freila's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Freila?
A: Freila offers a selection of 16 road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling in Freila?
A: Road cycling in Freila provides a diverse visual experience. You'll encounter the expansive blue waters of the Negratín Reservoir, dramatic reddish wastelands and semi-desert badlands like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4101272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorafe Desert&lt;/a&gt;, and fertile valleys dotted with olive groves. Prominent features like Cerro Jabalcón also offer rewarding views.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Freila?
A: Yes, while many routes offer moderate to difficult challenges, there is at least one route specifically categorized as easy. For those looking for a less strenuous ride, consider exploring the flatter sections around the Negratín Reservoir.

Q: Which routes offer continuous views of the Negratín Reservoir?
A: Several routes provide excellent views of the Negratín Reservoir. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350389541/pico-jabalcon-and-negratin-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Jabalcón and Negratín Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987052699/negratin-reservoir-loop-via-pozo-alcon-and-hinojares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares&lt;/a&gt; are both great options that showcase its turquoise waters and surrounding arid hills.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. Freila is known for its challenging terrain, with 9 routes categorized as difficult. These often include significant elevation gains, such as those found around Cerro Jabalcón or longer loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31814580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baza – Historic Center of Baza loop from Baza&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can encounter unique geological formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Coloraos Badlands&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6485845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoodoos of Cuevas del Campo&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3241591" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baúl Bridge&lt;/a&gt; is another point of interest. Many routes also pass through or near the historic town of Baza.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Freila?
A: Freila's climate makes it suitable for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling. Summers can be hot, especially in the semi-desert areas, while winters are milder but can still have cooler days.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, for cyclists seeking longer distances, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31814580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baza – Historic Center of Baza loop from Baza&lt;/a&gt; cover approximately 76.5 km (47.5 miles) and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987052699/negratin-reservoir-loop-via-pozo-alcon-and-hinojares" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Negratín Reservoir Loop via Pozo Alcón and Hinojares&lt;/a&gt; is about 62.9 km (39.1 miles), offering extended rides.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about Freila?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Freila, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the stunning views of the Negratín Reservoir, and the unique experience of riding through the badlands.

Q: Can I find loop routes for road cycling in Freila?
A: Many of the road cycling routes around Freila are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350389541/pico-jabalcon-and-negratin-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Jabalcón and Negratín Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31467244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Freila – Zújar loop from Zújar&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there road cycling routes that pass through historic towns?
A: Yes, some routes offer a cultural experience by passing through or near historic towns. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31814580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baza – Historic Center of Baza loop from Baza&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, allowing you to explore the charm of Baza.

Q: What are the typical durations for road cycling routes in Freila?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and elevation. Shorter, moderate loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1350389541/pico-jabalcon-and-negratin-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Jabalcón and Negratín Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; can take around 1 hour 30 minutes, while longer, more difficult routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31814580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baza – Historic Center of Baza loop from Baza&lt;/a&gt; may take nearly 4 hours.

Viewpoint from where you can see the town of Hinojares, a village sunken into the bottom of a valley and where it is surrounded by the famous "badlands" or Desierto de Hinojares.

Population that is divided into two parts, one on the plain and the other next to Loma de la Mesa. The part that is on the plain is guided by the Iglesia Parroquial Sagrada Familia, which is located just above the small stream that is in the core.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling trail, with about 61 km of 'Road' and 1.7 km of 'Street'. You'll find approximately 34 km of asphalt and 28 km of paved surfaces. However, it's important to note that some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride, so be prepared for varied conditions.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery and several highlights. You'll enjoy stunning views of the surreal turquoise waters of the Negratín Reservoir, which is the third largest in Andalusia. There's also the Hinojares Viewpoint, offering panoramic vistas of the reservoir, Jabalcón Hill, and the unique 'badlands' topography. The route also passes through the settlement of Fontanar and areas with olive groves and cave houses.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or does it require good fitness?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a moderate road cycling route. With an elevation gain of approximately 720 meters over 62.9 km, it features a rolling and hilly profile with sustained climbs. Good fitness is recommended to comfortably complete the ride, especially considering some potentially unpaved sections.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The trail is located in a region that can experience hot summers. For the most comfortable cycling experience, consider visiting during the spring or autumn months when temperatures are milder. This allows you to fully enjoy the scenery without extreme heat.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle in the Geoparque de Granada area?
faqs.answer: No, there are generally no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route within the Geoparque de Granada. The park is open for public access, but always respect local regulations, private property, and natural environments.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Plain of the Caves of Campo cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, as a circular route, you can typically start and park in one of the nearby villages such as Hinojares or Freila, which are often used as access points for routes in the Negratín Reservoir area. Look for public parking spaces within these settlements.

faqs.question: Is the Plain of the Caves of Campo route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling route. While dogs are generally allowed in public areas, cycling with dogs on a racebike route of this length and difficulty might be challenging. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are well-trained, can keep pace, and are accustomed to road cycling environments. Always keep them on a leash and be mindful of local regulations.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with trails such as the Camino de la Baza, Pista Retamar, and the Vereda del Camino Real de Andalucía, among others. These intersections are generally minor and part of the overall circular route.

faqs.question: What unique geological features can be seen along the way?
faqs.answer: The area is part of the Granada Geopark and is renowned for its unique 'badlands' topography and impressive geological formations. You can observe 'hoodoos' near Cuevas del Campo, which are natural stone pillars resembling those found in places like Cappadocia or Bryce Canyon, particularly visible around the Desierto de Hinojares.

faqs.question: Are there any places to get food or drinks along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through small villages like Hinojares, which features a social club popular with cyclists. While not abundant, these villages may offer opportunities for refreshments. It's always advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially given the route's length and the potentially arid landscape.

faqs.question: What should I wear or bring for this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: Given it's a racebike tour, wear appropriate cycling attire, including padded shorts and a helmet. Bring plenty of water, especially during warmer months, as well as snacks. Sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses) is essential due to the open landscape. A basic repair kit for your bike is also highly recommended.
